title: Crime Aid
weakest_section: 09:20-10:20 range with Michael's rambling auction explanation

jokes.text: Hey. * pamela-A-A *
jokes.setup: Michael calls Pam at her part-time corporate job
jokes.punchline: He sings her name in an overly familiar, inappropriate way
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael inappropriate boundary crossing with his sing-song delivery. Character-specific behavior that creates cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Pam? Paaaam? Pam, Pam, Pam, Paaaam!

jokes.text: Of course, Now michael knows where to reach me 16 to 18 hours a week.
jokes.setup: Pam got a part-time job through Michael
jokes.punchline: She realizes the unintended consequence of Michael having constant access to her
jokes.explanation: Good setup-payoff with Pam's dry delivery. The specific hour range makes it funnier than just 'part-time.'

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Michael gave me a job at the warehouse so I could make some extra money.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: But now he's down there all the time, and he keeps trying to hang out with me during my breaks.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I realized I can't escape him. He has constant access to me now.

jokes.text: Hey, michael. * pamela-A-A *
jokes.setup: Michael calls again immediately after Pam's confessional
jokes.punchline: He does the same sing-song name thing, proving her point
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate callback that validates Pam's complaint. Great comedic timing and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Pam? Pam? Pam?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: He's doing the sing-song thing again.

jokes.text: Um, tonight. I'm free tonight. Is that too eager? No. I don't care. I'm free tonight.
jokes.setup: Michael and Holly planning their third date
jokes.punchline: Michael's stream-of-consciousness reveals his desperation despite trying to play it cool
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Michael's inability to be smooth even when he tries.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So Holly, I'm thinking we could do something fun this time. Keep it light. No pressure.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I mean, I don't really care what we do. Doesn't matter to me. We could just sit in silence. That would be great. That would be the best night of my life.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I've already picked out baby names. I'm not attached to any of them, but I do have a list. Several lists actually.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: But yeah, whatever you want to do. I'm cool. Very cool. So cool.

jokes.text: Oh, wait. Oh, tonight's no good. Because i am busy taking you out. Oh, i just remembered. I can't tonight. Why? I'm going out with you.
jokes.setup: Michael and Holly trying to arrange their date
jokes.punchline: The conversation devolves into nonsensical back-and-forth where they keep saying they can't go out because they're going out
jokes.explanation: Escalating absurdist bit that builds nicely. Good structure even if it goes a bit long.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So can you go out tonight?

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: I can't, I'm going out.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You're going out? But I'm going out too.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Well I can't go out because I'm already going out.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: But we could go out together.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: I can't go out with you because I'm going out.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You're going out without me?

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Yes, and I can't go out with you because I'm going out.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: This doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: That's why I can't go out.

jokes.text: In my opinion, the third date is traditionally the one where... You have sex. Does holly feel that way? I don't know. I will probably find out tonight. If she starts having sex with me, I'll know for sure.
jokes.setup: Michael discussing third date expectations
jokes.punchline: His circular logic about knowing Holly's feelings based on whether she has sex with him
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael inappropriate overshare with faulty reasoning that sounds logical to him.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: If Holly sleeps with me on the third date, that means she really likes me. If she doesn't sleep with me on the third date, that means she's playing hard to get, which means she really likes me.

jokes.text: I am right in assuming that Dwight is short for d-Money. 'Cause that's what i wrote on your save-The-Date.
jokes.setup: Jim asking about Dwight's name
jokes.punchline: He's invented 'D-Money' as a nickname and already used it on wedding invitations
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jim prank setup. The commitment to using it on save-the-dates elevates a simple nickname joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So what do people call you? Like, do you have a nickname?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: D-Money.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: D-Money?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yeah. I've already had it printed on the wedding invitations.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You're not getting married.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Not yet.

jokes.text: J-Money. Or should it be t-Money, for tuna? Receptionist-Money. K-Money.
jokes.setup: Jim's D-Money joke
jokes.punchline: Dwight tries to turn it around but gets caught up in the bit, everyone joins in
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the money-name bit, though it loses steam as it goes on.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: D-Money!

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: D-Money!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's not even—

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: D-Money!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Fine. D-Money.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: D-Money!

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: D-Money!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: D-Money!

jokes.text: What are you making? A knife. You're making a knife with a knife? You got a better way?
jokes.setup: Jim notices Dwight making something
jokes.punchline: Dwight's matter-of-fact response to the circular logic of making a knife with a knife
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight logic delivered with complete sincerity. Economical and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Making a knife.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: With what?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A knife.

jokes.text: You wanna talk about it? About what? You know I know. You know they know. I know none of that. And if i did, you'd be the last to know.
jokes.setup: Jim trying to get Dwight to admit he knows about Jim and Pam
jokes.punchline: Escalating circular conversation about who knows what, ending with Dwight's defensive non-denial
jokes.explanation: Good escalating word play that reveals character dynamics without being too obvious.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know about me and Pam.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't know what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yes, you do. You know.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Know what? I know a lot of things.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: About me and Pam!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't know anything about you and Pam.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: But you know that I know that you know.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't know what you think I know, but I don't know it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So you admit you know something.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I admit nothing! False. I know nothing about your relationship with Pam, and I certainly don't care.

jokes.text: We could go to the food court, get different foods. You could get chicken teriyaki. I could get a hot dog. Some of what we order depends on whether we're having sex after.
jokes.setup: Michael planning their mall date
jokes.punchline: He matter-of-factly connects food choices to sexual expectations
jokes.explanation: Good Michael inappropriate overshare with practical planning twisted by sexual obsession.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: If I buy her lunch, she's gonna think I'm expecting something.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: If I don't buy her lunch, she's gonna think I'm a cheapskate.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: If I buy her a salad, she's gonna think I think she's fat.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: If I buy her a cheeseburger, she's gonna think I'm trying to get her pregnant.

jokes.text: Oh, my! Wow, elephant in the room. Are we, do you think? Do you think we're gonna have sex tonight? Hell, yeah.
jokes.setup: Michael brings up sex directly
jokes.punchline: Holly's enthusiastic 'Hell, yeah!' response
jokes.explanation: Holly's unexpected directness creates a good surprise. Shows they're well-matched in awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So, um, what do you say we get out of here and go back to my place?

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Hell, yeah!

jokes.text: She introduced me to so many things. Pasteurized milk, sheets, Monotheism...
jokes.setup: Dwight reflecting on what Angela taught him
jokes.punchline: List escalates from basic hygiene to fundamental religious concepts
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy revealing Dwight's primitive background. Perfect escalation from practical to profound.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Angela taught me so much. How to properly fold a fitted sheet. How to tell if produce is ripe just by looking at it. The existence of God.

jokes.text: Presents on your birthday, Preventative medicine. It's nice to learn new things.
jokes.setup: Continuing Dwight's list of Angela's civilizing influence
jokes.punchline: His sincere appreciation for basic human concepts, delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Great continuation that maintains the absurdity while adding pathos with his genuine gratitude.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Angela has taught me the importance of things like... personal hygiene, basic manners, and not eating directly from the trash can at work.

jokes.text: That's really fattening. No, it's lettuce.
jokes.setup: Angela commenting on someone's food
jokes.punchline: She calls lettuce fattening, revealing extreme dietary obsession
jokes.explanation: Perfect Angela character moment. The contrast between 'fattening' and 'lettuce' is economical and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Lettuce is fattening.

jokes.text: I'm a crazy 8. I'm crazy. You're crazy. Go crazy.
jokes.setup: Michael getting excited about their date timing
jokes.punchline: His awkward attempt at being spontaneous and fun comes across as manic
jokes.explanation: Weaker Michael moment that feels forced rather than naturally awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, okay. So we're going out tonight, right? And I'm thinking, why do we have to plan everything? Why can't we just be spontaneous? Why can't we just... go with the flow?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So I'm gonna do something crazy. I'm gonna spin this globe, and wherever my finger lands, that's where we're going. Ready?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: WHOA! WHOA! Okay, okay, I'm doing it, I'm doing it, I'm feeling it, I'm feeling the spontaneity coursing through my veins right now!

jokes.text: Oh, after vous. Thank vous.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to be gallant
jokes.punchline: His mangled French with 'thank vous' instead of 'merci'
jokes.explanation: Simple Michael malapropism. Gets the job done but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Thank vous.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: It's 'merci,' Michael.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know what I said.

jokes.text: Oh! I didn't forget my keys. I just didn't want to make out with you In front of the cameras.
jokes.setup: Michael pretending he forgot his keys
jokes.punchline: He reveals his actual motive while talking directly to the cameras he's trying to avoid
jokes.explanation: Good meta-joke that plays with the documentary format while revealing character.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I can't find my keys. They're just... gone. I have no idea where they are.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I guess I'm just stuck here. Looks like I'll have to stay late and work on those reports that are due tomorrow.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Such a shame, really. I was really looking forward to going home and... not doing anything fun.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I mean, it's not like I'm avoiding Jan or anything. That would be ridiculous.

jokes.text: Do you think they can hear us? Not if we turn these dials All the way down. Now they can't hear us at all. Oh, good. We're totally alone.
jokes.setup: Michael and Holly trying to get privacy from cameras
jokes.punchline: They think turning down random dials makes them inaudible while we hear everything
jokes.explanation: Great visual comedy with dramatic irony - audience knows they're wrong but characters are confident.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Holly, I think if we just turn down these dials, the cameras won't be able to hear us.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: That's... that's not how this works, Michael.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm pretty sure it does. See? I'm turning it down.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: You're just making it quieter for everyone watching.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Perfect. Now they can't hear anything we say.

jokes.text: Great, they stole my laptop. Yeah, well, they stole my surge protector. How does that even compare? Oscar, i'm now going to be prone to surges.
jokes.setup: Office discussing what was stolen in robbery
jokes.punchline: Kevin's completely disproportionate concern about surge protection versus laptop theft
jokes.explanation: Good Kevin logic - missing the big picture while fixating on minor technical detail with sincere worry.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Wait, did anyone check if the surge protector was stolen?

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Kevin, someone stole a laptop.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Yeah, but those surge protectors are like thirty bucks!

jokes.text: Bravo, Watson. Looks like a classic seven-Man job.
jokes.setup: Office was robbed
jokes.punchline: Dwight immediately goes into detective mode with over-specific criminal analysis
jokes.explanation: Typical Dwight overconfident expertise. The 'seven-man' specificity is funny but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A break-in. Probably around 2 AM. The perpetrator knew the layout of the building. They were familiar with our security protocols. Height: approximately 5'10". Build: athletic but not muscular. Shoes: size 11, possibly 12. They work alone. And they're left-handed.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: How could you possibly know all that?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm a student of the game, Jim.

jokes.text: Oh, my god! So much for sex without consequences. You are such a dork!
jokes.setup: Michael realizes they may have left the office unlocked
jokes.punchline: He immediately connects their romantic evening to the robbery as 'consequences'
jokes.explanation: Good Michael moment connecting unrelated events. Holly calling him a dork shows their dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh no, did we leave the office unlocked?

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: I don't know, why?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Because if we did, someone could have broken in and stolen everything. This is all because of us. Because we were together.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Michael, that's not—

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no. These are the consequences of our love, Holly. This is what happens.

jokes.text: So apparently Pam went out last night And accidentally called my work phone at 3:00 in the morning. I'm not drunk. I'm on minute six of this message.
jokes.setup: Jim got a drunk voicemail from Pam
jokes.punchline: The juxtaposition of her denial with the evidence of a 6+ minute rambling message
jokes.explanation: Good structure contrasting denial with evidence. Relatable drunk-dial scenario.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: She left me this voicemail where she's like 'I'm not drunk,' and then it's just six minutes of her rambling about... I don't even know what.

jokes.text: Okay, i do not sound like that. You can take the girl out of philly... Scranton. The future mother of my children.
jokes.setup: Jim listening to Pam's drunk voicemail
jokes.punchline: His affectionate corrections of her mistakes and his sincere 'future mother' comment
jokes.explanation: Sweet Jim moment showing his love through gentle mockery. The Philly/Scranton correction is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hey Pam, it's me. I got your voicemail. You said you were at Chili's with the girls, but actually you're at TGI Friday's. Also, you pronounced 'margarita' wrong—it's 'marga-REE-ta,' not 'marga-REE-tuh.' And when you said you'd 'love me forever,' that was really sweet. You're going to make an amazing mother someday.

jokes.text: I'm a very good screamer.
jokes.setup: Dwight says she's always safe with him
jokes.punchline: Angela's unexpected claim about being good at screaming
jokes.explanation: Unexpected line from prim Angela. Economy of words with maximum surprise factor.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I'm actually very good at screaming. I took a class.

jokes.text: It did when Bob said I had to stop talking to my sister on the phone so much.
jokes.setup: Phyllis advising Dwight to give Angela an ultimatum
jokes.punchline: She casually reveals Bob gave her an ultimatum about talking to her sister
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy revealing controlling relationship dynamics delivered with Phyllis's casual tone.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: You should give her an ultimatum, Dwight. That's what Bob did with me. He said, 'Either stop talking to your sister or we're done.'

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: And what did you do?

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Well, I'm still married to Bob, aren't I?

jokes.text: The last person to do this disappeared. His name? Creed Bratton.
jokes.setup: Creed threatens whoever stole from him
jokes.punchline: The disappeared person was... also named Creed Bratton, implying identity theft
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Creed moment with perfect misdirection. Implies he stole someone's identity while threatening thieves. Quotable and mysterious.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Whoever stole from me better watch their back. I know people. I've done things.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Actually, I just found out who it was. Some guy named Creed Bratton.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Never heard of him. But when I find him, he's gonna pay.

jokes.text: It's like farm aid. But instead of farms fighting against aids, It is us fighting against our own poverty
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his Crime-Aid charity auction concept
jokes.punchline: He completely misunderstands what Farm Aid was about, thinking farms were fighting AIDS
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael misunderstanding delivered with complete confidence. The AIDS/aid confusion is brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what Farm Aid was? Farms fighting AIDS. That's what we're doing here, but with crime.

jokes.text: Was this a sorority that you didn't get into a real sorority So you had to kind of form your own?
jokes.setup: Karen mentions her sorority did charity auctions
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately assumes it was a fake sorority, insulting her to her face
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael inappropriate question. Creates cringe but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. A fake sorority? That's not a real thing, is it?

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: No, it was a real sorority. We did charity auctions.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh, come on. No offense, but those aren't real sororities. Those are like, you know, made-up things for girls who couldn't get into real ones.

jokes.text: We'll auction off people, Like in the olden days.
jokes.setup: Michael brainstorming auction ideas
jokes.punchline: He casually suggests slavery as a fundraising model
jokes.explanation: Dark Michael moment but relies on shock value more than cleverness.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: What if we did a slave auction? You know, like people bid on other people to do chores for them? We could raise a ton of money!

jokes.text: It could be a pedestrian. It could be a old person. It could be a lookie-Loo. Or it could be A bruce springsteen fan-- What? Who said that? I did. Why did i say that? Oh, i think you know why i said that.
jokes.setup: Michael listing who might come to the auction
jokes.punchline: His stream-of-consciousness about Springsteen fans becomes a confusing monologue where he questions his own words
jokes.explanation: Rambling Michael bit that doesn't build to anything satisfying. Too unfocused to be effective.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know who's gonna come to this auction? Bruce Springsteen fans. They're crazy. They follow him around. They know every song. Every word. Every... I don't even know what they know. But they know it. And they'll pay for it. They'll pay a lot of money for a Bruce Springsteen... what are we selling? A guitar? A shirt? I don't even remember. But they'll want it. They want everything. These people, they're dedicated. You gotta respect that. Although, is that healthy? Following someone around like that? Is that... I mean, I follow people. Not like, physically follow them. But I'm aware of them. I know what they're doing. That's not weird, right?

jokes.text: Michael scored the big ticket item. Springsteen tickets. The boss scored the boss. Yeah, i think that's pretty boss.
jokes.setup: Michael got Springsteen tickets for the auction
jokes.punchline: His terrible wordplay connecting 'boss' (Springsteen's nickname) with 'boss' (good)
jokes.explanation: Weak Michael wordplay. The pun is forced and not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I got Springsteen tickets for the auction. You know why? Because he's the Boss. And I'm the boss. And I'm a good boss. So it's like, we're both the Boss. It's perfect.

jokes.text: Listen to me close, 'Cause i'm only gonna say this once. You either break off your engagement with that singing buffoon, apologize,and then date me. Or you can say good-Bye to this.
jokes.setup: Dwight giving Angela an ultimatum
jokes.punchline: His overly dramatic delivery and calling Andy a 'singing buffoon' while presumably gesturing to himself
jokes.explanation: Good Dwight dramatic moment. 'Singing buffoon' is a nice character-specific insult.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Angela, you have to choose. It's me or Andy. And frankly, I don't see how this is even a choice. I'm a beet farmer, a volunteer sheriff's deputy, and I have a black belt in multiple martial arts. What does Andy have? A perfect pitch and the ability to annoy everyone in this office. The man is nothing but a singing buffoon.

jokes.text: Crime reduces innocence. Makes everyone angry,I declare.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining what C.R.I.M.E.A.I.D. stands for
jokes.punchline: His forced acronym makes no sense and he has to declare it rather than it being natural
jokes.explanation: Good Michael forced acronym humor. The 'I declare' adds to the awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: C.R.I.M.E.A.I.D. stands for... Crimes... Reduce... Injuries... Making... Employees... Avoid... Injuries... Daily.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's an acronym.

jokes.text: Mike gave me a list of his top ten springsteen songs. Three of them were huey lewis and the news. One was tracy chapman, fast car. And my personal favorite, Short people.
jokes.setup: Toby listing Michael's supposed Springsteen songs
jokes.punchline: The escalating wrongness of the artists, ending with Randy Newman's 'Short People'
jokes.explanation: Great escalating list showing Michael's musical ignorance. Each wrong artist gets funnier, especially 'Short People.'

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: Michael claims Bruce Springsteen wrote 'I Will Follow You Into the Dark' by Death Cab for Cutie.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: He also says Springsteen wrote 'Wonderwall' by Oasis.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: And apparently 'Bitter Sweet Symphony' by The Verve.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: But the worst one? He insists Randy Newman's 'Short People' is a Bruce Springsteen deep cut.

jokes.text: It squeaks when you bang it. That's what she said.
jokes.setup: Michael's makeshift gavel squeaks
jokes.punchline: Perfect setup for Michael's signature catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for the catchphrase. Not original but well-executed within the show's context.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

jokes.text: It just says creed. Yeah, that's all-Inclusive.
jokes.setup: Auction item description is just 'Creed'
jokes.punchline: Creed's matter-of-fact explanation that this covers everything about him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed mystery. The ambiguity of what 'all-inclusive' means is both funny and unsettling.

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: We have an auction item here. It's just listed as 'Creed.'

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: That's right. That covers everything.

jokes.text: She thought I was mcnabb. I can see that.
jokes.setup: Darryl at the bar with Jim and others
jokes.punchline: Someone mistook him for Eagles quarterback McNabb, Jim's diplomatic response
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor. Jim's polite response to potentially awkward racial comparison.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Some guy thought I was Donovan McNabb.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, you know, that's... that's a compliment. He's an excellent quarterback.

jokes.text: Hi, i'm kevin. And i'll do your taxes. Let's hear an opening bid, everybody. Who's first? Kevin do your taxes. He's the tax man. Kevin, the tax man. Federal and state.
jokes.setup: Kevin being auctioned for tax services
jokes.punchline: The complete lack of bidding interest despite Michael's enthusiastic pitch
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Kevin's incompetence through audience non-response.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Kevin Brown, everyone. He's a fantastic accountant. Great with numbers. Very detail-oriented. Who wants to start the bidding at $500?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Nobody? Okay, $250?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Come on, people. He'll do your taxes. Very reasonably.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Do I hear $50?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Michael, maybe we should just—

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Sold! To nobody! For nothing!

jokes.text: Fine. Okay, all right. Hey, you know what? I would appreciate it if people would stop storming off the stage.
jokes.setup: Kevin presumably leaves the stage after no bids
jokes.punchline: Michael's exasperated request treating Kevin's exit as dramatic when it's just awkward
jokes.explanation: Good reaction to the awkwardness, though not particularly strong.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Kevin, Kevin! Come back! We're not done yet!

jokes.text: Someone let the air out of your tires. Come quick. Oh, dear.
jokes.setup: Dwight needs to get Phyllis away from the auction
jokes.punchline: He actually let the air out of her tires to create the emergency he's reporting
jokes.explanation: Good Dwight scheming moment. The commitment to actually damaging property for his plan is perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Phyllis, there's an emergency with your car in the parking lot. You need to go check on it right now.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: What? What kind of emergency?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't have time to explain. Just go!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I let the air out of her tires.

jokes.text: And you slap like a girl.
jokes.setup: Dwight and Phyllis arguing after his ultimatum failed
jokes.punchline: His parting shot is a childish insult
jokes.explanation: Weak Dwight moment. Generic insult that doesn't feel clever or particularly character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Well, I hope you're happy with yourself, Phyllis. You've made a grave mistake.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Oh please, Dwight. Your ultimatum didn't work.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You're... you're a big fat jerk!

jokes.text: To me.
jokes.setup: Roy asks if Pam is engaged
jokes.punchline: Jim's simple, matter-of-fact revelation delivered with perfect timing
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing with maximum economy. Two words that completely shift the conversation's dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Is Pam engaged?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: I thought you were a friend.
jokes.setup: Jim reveals he's engaged to Pam
jokes.punchline: Roy's hurt response thinking Jim was being friendly while 'stealing' his ex
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Roy's perspective, though not particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Wait, you're marrying Pam? I thought we were friends, man. You were supposed to be my friend!

jokes.text: Oh, where are those? Has anyone seen An envelope with Bruce Springsteen Front row tickets And backstage passes?
jokes.setup: Michael ready to auction the big ticket item
jokes.punchline: He's lost the tickets he's been promoting all episode
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael incompetence at the worst moment. Good dramatic timing for the episode's climax.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Alright everyone, we've come to the main event of the evening. The item we've all been waiting for. I have lost the tickets.

jokes.text: I have my hug. Yeah, well, phyllis, Nobody...really wants a hug, so.
jokes.setup: Phyllis pointing out she has something to auction
jokes.punchline: Michael's brutal dismissal of her offering
jokes.explanation: Mean Michael moment that creates appropriate cringe but isn't particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I have something to auction.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, you don't.

jokes.text: She's your wife, you idiot. $100. 200. What are you doing? I need a hug, unless you're gonna give me one. Not here.
jokes.setup: Bob bids on his wife's hug
jokes.punchline: Michael's confusion followed by Bob's practical explanation and Phyllis's response
jokes.explanation: Good escalating absurdity with the married couple bidding dynamic. Bob's logic is sound but absurd in context.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, why are you bidding on your own wife's hug?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, I wanted to make sure I got it. You never know who might outbid you.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I would've given you a hug for free, Bob.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yeah, but now it's official.

jokes.text: $1 million. 700. 700 and one penny. Humuna-Humuna-Humuna- Humuna-Humuna-Humuna--
jokes.setup: Bidding war escalating between Bob and someone else
jokes.punchline: Michael's auctioneer excitement devolves into nonsense sounds
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy moment with Michael getting overwhelmed. The 'humuna' sounds are appropriately silly.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Going once, going twice! We've got 50, do I hear 60? 60, 70, 80, 90, 100! Going once, going twice, going three times!

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Sold! SOLD! Going, going, boing boing boing! Chicka chicka bow wow! Meep meep meep!

jokes.text: No. You know what? No. Because... I'm not that guy. And we are not that couple.
jokes.setup: Jim and Roy's tense encounter
jokes.punchline: Jim's mature refusal to engage in drama or conflict
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Jim's growth and maturity. The repetition of 'not that' emphasizes his point.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Look, Roy, I'm not going to fight you. That's not who I am.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Oh, so you think you're better than me?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, I just... I'm not going to do this. Not here, not now. It's not worth it.

jokes.text: The springsteen tickets seemed too good to be true. But a lot of michael seems too good to be true. So far it's all true. But yeah, those tickets Really seemed too good to be true.
jokes.setup: Holly reflecting on Michael's claims about having Springsteen tickets
jokes.punchline: Her circular logic about Michael being unbelievable but believable, except for this one thing
jokes.explanation: Good Holly logic showing her understanding of Michael while maintaining some skepticism. The repetition works well.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Michael said he has Bruce Springsteen tickets. Now, Michael lies about a lot of things, so normally I wouldn't believe him. But he's also very convincing when he lies, so I do believe him. Except for this. I don't believe this.

tmdb_overview: Michael and Holly have sex for the first time, and Michael holds a fund-raiser for the office after it is burglarized. Dwight confides in Phyllis after Andy and Angela set a wedding date, and Jim runs into Pam's ex-fiancé Roy.
insight: Cringe escalation drives a 79-point episode without standout one-liners.
